Running title:  Isolation of neuronal mitochondria from human cortex
Key words:  neurons; mitochondria; human cortex; isolation; percoll; synaptosome; non-synaptosomal 
mitochondria; differential centrifugation; purified; functionalPage 3 of 17Accepted Manuscript
3Abstract
Background: Functional and structural properties of mitochondria are highly tissue and cell 
dependent, but isolation of highly purified human neuronal mitochondria is not currently 
available New Method: We developed and validated a procedure to isolate purified neuronal mitochondria 
from brain tissue The method combines Percoll gradient centrifugation to obtain synaptosomal 
fraction with nitrogen cavitation mediated synaptosome disruption and extraction of 
mitochondria using anti mitochondrial outer membrane protein antibodies conjugated to 
magnetic beads The final products of isolation are non-synaptosomal mitochondria, which are a 
mixture of mitochondria isolated from different brain cells (i.e neurons, astrocytes, 
oligodendrocytes, microglia) and synaptic mitochondria, which are of neuronal origin This 
method is well suited for preparing functional mitochondria from human cortex tissue that is 
surgically extracted 
Results: The procedure produces mitochondria with minimal cytoplasmic contaminations that are 
functionally active based on measurements of mitochondrial respiration as well as mitochondrial 
protein import The procedure requires approximately four hours for the isolation of human 
neuronal mitochondria and can also be used to isolate mitochondria from mouse/rat/monkey 
brains 
Comparison with Existing Methods and Conclusions: This method will allow researchers to 
study highly enriched neuronal mitochondria without the confounding effect of cellular and 
organelle contaminants Page 4 of 17Accepted Manuscript
4Introduction
Mitochondria play an important role in neuronal homeostasis by providing energy and 
participating in numerous signaling pathways(Zhang  et al  2013, Yano  et al  2014, Friedlander 
2003, Hensley & Harris-White 2015, Wang  et al 2008) Mitochondrial function has been shown 
to be different in various tissues and cell types(Carafoli & Lehninger 1971, Zhang et al 2013, 
Teng  et al  2004, Zhou  et al  2014) Given that mitochondrial dysfunction plays a critical role in 
neurodegeneration, it is of outmost importance to accurately evaluate highly purified functional 
neuronal mitochondria(Zhu  et al  2002, Zhang  et al  2008, Kristal  et al  2004) Isolation of 
highly purified functional neuronal mitochondria has been very challenging due to the 
heterogeneity of brain tissue The only method shown to be able to provide neuronal 
mitochondria involves their extraction from synaptosomes Synaptosomes are vesicles formed 
from neuronal processes during mechanical homogenization of brain tissue and encapsulate 
neuronal mitochondria It has been shown that synaptosomal mitochondria respond differently to 
stressors such as Ca2+ overload as compared with non-synaptosomal mitochondria(Brown  et al 2006) Our group has recently reported that mitochondrial protein import is impaired exclusively 
in synaptosomal (or neuronal) mitochondria of presymptomatic R6/2 mice, a mouse model of 
Huntington’s disease (HD) (Yano et al 2014)

mitochondria.protocol:
**Steps:**

1. Combined Percoll gradient centrifugation and anti-TOM22 magnetic bead extraction is used to isolate mitochondria.
2. The mitochondria obtained are neuronal, obtained from synaptosome disruption.
3. The mitochondria obtained have minimal cytoplasmic contaminants, including plasma membrane, peroxisomes, lysosomes, synaptosomes, and endoplasmic reticulum.
4. The mitochondria are functionally active based on measurements of respiration and protein import.

**Conclusion:**

The article describes a method for isolating functionally active and highly purified neuronal mitochondria from human cortex. The method involves using Percoll gradient centrifugation and anti-TOM22 magnetic bead extraction. The obtained mitochondria are neuronal, have minimal cytoplasmic contaminants, and are functionally active. However, the article does not provide a detailed step-by-step protocol for the isolation procedure.

Summary 4:
Researchers have developed and validated a method to isolate functionally active and highly purified neuronal mitochondria from the human cortex. The method combines Percoll gradient centrifugation and anti-TOM22 magnetic bead extraction. The isolated mitochondria are neuronal and have minimal cytoplasmic contaminants. They are functionally active, as demonstrated by measurements of mitochondrial respiration and protein import. The procedure takes approximately four hours and can also be used to isolate mitochondria from mouse, rat, and monkey brains. This method allows for the study of highly enriched neuronal mitochondria without the confounding effects of cellular and organelle contaminants.

Chunk # 1.
It is of interest that primary neurons and cell lines 
expressing mutant huntingtin demonstrated a mitochondrial protein import defect as well (Yano 
et al 2014) The most robust protocols to isolate synaptosomal mitochondria include a Percoll/Ficoll 
discontinuous gradient centrifugation step followed by synaptosomal disruption with either 
digitonin or nitrogen cavitation pressurization(Sims & Anderson 2008, Kristian 2010, Hansson  et 
al 2008, Barksdale  et al  2010) Neither method, however, allows for the elimination of 
contaminants, including synaptic vesicles, lysosomes, and peroxisomes Whole synaptosomes 
are also still found in the final mitochondrial fraction using these methods(Kristian 2010) To 
mitigate this obstacle, we used specific anti-mitochondrial outer membrane protein TOM22 
antibodies to purify synaptosomal mitochondria without loss of function These antibodies are 
conjugated with magnetic microbeads for use in magnetic activated cell sorting (MACS) Using 
a magnetic field, the microbeads are able to selectively retain mitochondria as they bind to the 
TOM22 antibodies Any non-mitochondrial fraction of the sample will therefore be washed out 
The exclusive use of the MACS technique was shown to be sufficient to obtain brain 
mitochondria from a mixture of cell types (neuronal body, glia) but not pure neuronal 
mitochondria because it requires disruption of the synaptosomes before application of the 
antibodies The method described in this report yields highly purified and functional neuronal 
mitochondria We successfully characterized the mitochondrial status of human cortical neurons 
using mitochondria obtained from this protocol Previous reports described brain mitochondria 
isolation(Hansson  et al  2011) To our knowledge, this is the first reported protocol to isolate 
highly pure functional neuronal mitochondria from adult human cortex This report describes the 
isolation of pure synaptosomal/neuronal mitochondria It also yields highly purified non-
synaptosomal mitochondria that can be used for functional or structural analyses Materials

Mitochondria were isolated from different fresh brain tissues including human and mouse The

mitochondria.protocol:
1. The most robust protocols to isolate synaptosomal mitochondria include a Percoll/Ficoll discontinuous gradient centrifugation step followed by synaptosomal disruption with either digitonin or nitrogen cavitation pressurization.
2. Whole synaptosomes are still found in the final mitochondrial fraction using these methods.
3. To mitigate this obstacle, specific anti-mitochondrial outer membrane protein TOM22 antibodies are used to purify synaptosomal mitochondria without loss of function. These antibodies are conjugated with magnetic microbeads for use in magnetic activated cell sorting (MACS).
4. Using a magnetic field, the microbeads selectively retain mitochondria as they bind to the TOM22 antibodies, washing out any non-mitochondrial fraction of the sample.
5. The exclusive use of the MACS technique was shown to be sufficient to obtain brain mitochondria from a mixture of cell types, but not pure neuronal mitochondria because it requires disruption of the synaptosomes before application of the antibodies.
6. The method described in this report yields highly purified and functional neuronal mitochondria.
7. Surgical extracted fresh brain tissue is used for the isolation of mitochondria.
8. The tissue is placed in ice-cold Isolation Buffer 1 (IB-1) and rinsed to remove blood.
9. The washed tissue is transferred to a beaker with fresh IB-1.
10. The tissue is minced into small pieces using scissors to eliminate trapped blood.
11. The minced tissue is transferred to a glass potter.
12. The tissue is homogenized using 20 slow up and down strokes with a Teflon pestle operated at 400 rpm. The volume of added IB-1 should be 5 to 10 times the estimated volume of the tissue sample.

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The article does not provide the complete protocol for isolating synaptosomal mitochondria using Percoll/Ficoll discontinuous gradient centrifugation.
- The article does not provide details on the synaptosomal disruption step with digitonin or nitrogen cavitation pressurization.
- The article does not provide information on the specific concentrations or volumes of the reagents used in the isolation buffer or the Percoll solutions.
- The article does not provide information on the specific temperature or duration of the homogenization step.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a general overview of the protocol for isolating synaptosomal mitochondria using Percoll/Ficoll discontinuous gradient centrifugation and synaptosomal disruption. However, it lacks specific details on the centrifugation and disruption steps, as well as the concentrations and volumes of reagents used. The protocol also requires the use of TOM22 antibodies and magnetic activated cell sorting (MACS) to purify synaptosomal mitochondria. Overall, the protocol is incomplete and further information is needed to replicate it accurately.

mitochondria.protocol:
Mitochondrial Isolation Protocol:

1. Slowly homogenize the brain specimen, ensuring a low pressure is induced during the upward movement of the pestle to avoid disruption of the mitochondria.
2. If the volume of the brain specimen is too large, perform multiple homogenizations.
3. Perform all isolation steps at 4 °C.
4. Transfer the homogenate to a 16 mL or 50 mL centrifuge tube, depending on the final volume.
5. Centrifuge the tube at 1,300 x g for 3 min at 4 °C.
6. Carefully decant the supernatant into an empty polycarbonate centrifuge tube and place it on ice.
7. The pellet should be loose and easily disrupted. Re-suspend the pellet in 5-10 mL of IB-1.
8. Centrifuge the suspension at 1,300 x g for 3 min at 4 °C again.
9. Repeat steps 4-8 as many times as necessary based on the total volume of the brain specimen.
10. Centrifuge the pooled supernatant at 21,700 x g for 10 min at 4 °C. Ensure no loose pellet is transferred to avoid compromising mitochondrial activity.
11. Prepare a Percoll gradient by creating a discontinuous layer of 1.5 mL of 40% Percoll at the bottom of a tube, with 4 mL of 24% Percoll layered on top.
12. Discard the supernatant and re-suspend the pellet in 15% Percoll.
13. Gently transfer 3.5 mL of the re-suspended pellet in the 15% Percoll on top of the 24% Percoll to create a third discontinuous layer.
14. Centrifuge at 30,700 x g for 8 min at 4 °C using slow acceleration/deceleration regimes.
15. Collect the synaptosomal fraction (SN) between the 15% and 24% layers of Percoll.
16. Collect the non-synaptosomal fraction (NS) of mitochondria between the 24% and 40% layers of Percoll.
17. Dilute the SN fraction at a 1:1 ratio with IB-1 and incubate in the cell disruption vessel at 1,100 psi for 15 min at 4 °C using a magnetic stirrer. Do not exceed 1,100 psi to maintain synaptosomal mitochondria amount/activity.
18. Adjust the volume of the NS fraction to 10-12 mL and centrifuge at 16,700 x g for 10 min at 4 °C.
19. Carefully aspirate the supernatant and re-suspend the loose pellet in 10 mL of IB-1.
20. Centrifuge at 6,900 x g for 10 min at 4 °C.
21. Decant the supernatant and re-suspend the pellet in 1 mL of IB-2. Centrifuge at 8,000 x g for 10 min at 4 °C.
22. Decant the supernatant and re-suspend the pellet. The NS mitochondria in the collected suspension are ready for further experiments.

- Slow pestle movement during homogenization prevents disruption of mitochondria by inducing low pressure.
- Multiple homogenizations are necessary if the brain specimen volume is too large.
- Perform all isolation steps at 4°C.
- Centrifuge the homogenate at 1,300 x g for 3 min at 4°C and decant the supernatant.
- Resuspend the loose pellet in IB-1 and centrifuge again.
- Repeat centrifugation steps as needed based on brain specimen volume.
- Centrifuge the pooled supernatant at 21,700 x g for 10 min at 4°C.
- Prepare a Percoll gradient with 40% and 24% layers.
- Discard supernatant, resuspend pellet in 15% Percoll, and transfer to the top of the 24% Percoll layer.
- Centrifuge at 30,700 x g for 8 min at 4°C.
- Collect synaptosomal fraction between 15% and 24% layers and non-synaptosomal fraction between 24% and 40% layers of Percoll.
- Dilute synaptosomal fraction with IB-1 and incubate at 1,100 psi for 15 min at 4°C.
- Adjust volume of non-synaptosomal fraction and centrifuge.
- Resuspend pellet in IB-1 and centrifuge.
- Resuspend pellet in IB-2 and centrifuge.
- Resuspend pellet in minimal volume of IB-2 for NS mitochondria.
- Collect SN fraction and centrifuge.
- Resuspend loose pellet in 10 mL of IB-1.
- Centrifuge and resuspend pellet in 1 mL of IB-2.
- SN mitochondria are ready for further experiments.
- Purify synaptosomal mitochondrial fraction using MACS Isolation Kit.
- Incubate disrupted SN fraction with anti-TOM22 microbeads.
- Wash LC column and apply mitochondrial suspension.
- Wash column and elute mitochondria with IB-2.

Chunk # 3.
 To increase the yield of mitochondria, repeat steps E and F once or twice until the 
flushed liquid out of the column becomes clear g) Wash the collected fraction of pure SN mitochondria twice using IB-2 by centrifuging 
at 13,000 x g for 4 min at 4 °C This step is important to remove the separation buffer as 
its composition affects subsequent mitochondrial studies Re-suspend the mitochondria in 
IB-2 using 20 μL of IB -2 buffer (as little as possible) It is important that mitochondria 
remain in high concentration suspension to maintain proper functionality h) Transfer the mitochondrial suspension into a 1 mL test tube and store on ice until used 
for functional tests, no longer than 4 h Otherwise, the sample may be stored at -80 °C or 
in liquid nitrogen ResultsPage 8 of 17Accepted Manuscript
8This method is specifically focused to provide highly enriched neuronal mitochondria that 
exhibit good functional activity To ensure that the yielded mitochondria fulfill these criteria, 
both synaptosomal and non-synaptosomal mitochondria were tested for purity and functionality 
Avoiding the presence of BSA in the final mitochondrial sample improves the analysis of 
mitochondrial proteins It is important to note that the isolation of highly purified synaptosomal 
mitochondria requires an additional 2 hours of processing as compared with non-synaptosomal 
mitochondria Assessment of mitochondrial purity
Purity of isolated mitochondria was tested using immunoblotting against known markers for 
mitochondrial and cytosolic fractions Ten µg of protein obtained from the different isolation 
methods (Percoll alone or Percoll plus MACS or samples from whole brain tissue lysate) were 
resolved by 4 -12 % gradient SDS–PAGE and transferred onto polyvinylidene fluoride-FL 
(Millipore, cat no IPFL-00010) membranes using a wet transfer apparatus (Bio-Rad; Hercules, 
CA) at 40 mA overnight at 4 °C Nonspecific antibody binding was blocked with Odyssey® 
Blocking Buffer (Li-Cor, cat no 927-40000) at room temperature for 1 h The PVDF-FL 
membrane was then incubated with primary antibodies (1:1000 dilution in the Odyssey® 
Blocking Buffer) at 40C overnight The primary antibodies used included total OXPHOS Rodent 
WB antibody Cocktail (Abcam, cat no ab110413) which is a mixture of five antibodies against 
different components of the oxidative phosphorylation chain: Respiratory Complex I, subunit 
NDUFB8 20 kDa (ab110242), Respiratory Complex II, subunit SDHB 30kDa (ab14714), 
Respiratory Complex III-Core protein 2, subunit UQCRC2 50 kDa (ab14745), Respiratory 
Complex IV, COX I (ab14705) and ATP-synthase (Complex V), alpha subunit ATP5A 
(ab14748) Other antibodies used were anti- α-Tubulin (Sigma, cat no T5168), anti-
Synaptophysin (Sigma, cat no S5768), anti-calreticulin (Abcam, cat no ab2907), anti-PEX-14 
(Abcam, cat no ab113286) and anti-LAMP1 (Abcam, cat no ab25630) After washing with 
phosphate-buffered saline, the membranes were further incubated with Goat anti-mouse or anti-
rabbit infrared-labeled IRDye® secondary antibodies (Li-Cor) (1:30,000 dilution in Odyssey® 
Blocking Buffer) at room temperature for 1 h Immunoreactive protein bands were visualized on 
Odyssey CLx imager (Li-Cor) and quantitated using the provided Image Studio software Figure 
2 shows that Percoll-isolated synaptosomal mitochondria retain substantial amounts of 
synaptophysin, a marker of synaptic vesicles and calreticulin, which is a marker of endoplasmic 
reticulum contamination Additional purification of the same Percoll-isolated synaptosomal 
mitochondria with the MACS apparatus decreased synaptophysin, calreticulin, PEX-14 (data not 
shown) and LAMP-1 (data not shown) contamination below detection limits Given the limited 
availability of surgically extracted human cortex, the purity blots were limited to whole brain 
samples, non-synaptosomal mitochondria and MACS-purified synaptosomal mitochondria  
Assessment of mitochondrial functionality 
Functionality of isolated mitochondria was assessed by measuring both mitochondrial respiratory 
function and mitochondrial protein import 
Mitochondrial respirationPage 9 of 17Accepted Manuscript
9Measurements were performed using a Clark type electrode using an OROBOROS Oxygraph-2k 
(Oroboros, Innsbruck, Austria) (Gnaiger 2001) at 30 °C Respiration of mitochondria (0.125 mg 
of protein/mL for nonsynaptosomal and 0.2 mg/mL for synaptosomal) was recorded in medium 
contained 5mM HEPES, 125 mM KCl, 2 mM KH 2PO 4, 5 mM MgCl 2, 0.02 mM EDTA, and 0.2 
mg/mL BSA (fatty acid free), pH 7.4 Respiratory substrates were used to feed electrons into the 
respiratory chain either via complex I (5 mM malate +5 mM glutamate) or via complex II (5 mM 
succinate) The respiratory control ratios (RCR) were calculated by dividing the rate of 
respiration in the presence of respiratory substrates (resting state respiration, V2) and the rate of 
respiration in the presence of both respiratory substrates with the addition of 120 µM ADP 
(ADP-stimulated respiration, V3) Oligomycin was then added to inhibit the mitochondrial ATP-
synthase (Complex V) Maximum respiratory capacity was measured in the presence of 
respiratory substrates and FCCP (1 µM), which is a mitochondrial respiration uncoupler 
Figure 3,A demonstrates that isolated mitochondria responded normally to an addition of ADP 
and FCCP Respiratory control ratios (RCR) are shown on the table

4. Transfer the mitochondrial suspension into a 1 mL test tube and store on ice until used for functional tests, no longer than 4 h. Alternatively, store the sample at -80 °C or in liquid nitrogen.

summary: Summary 6: To increase mitochondrial yield, repeat steps E and F until the flushed liquid is clear. Wash the collected pure SN mitochondria fraction twice with IB-2 by centrifuging at 13,000 x g for 4 min at 4 °C to remove the separation buffer. Resuspend the mitochondria in IB-2 using 20 μL of IB-2 buffer. Transfer the mitochondrial suspension into a 1 mL test tube and store on ice for up to 4 hours or at -80 °C or in liquid nitrogen for long-term storage. This method provides highly enriched neuronal mitochondria with good functional activity. Purity and functionality were assessed using immunoblotting against known markers for mitochondrial and cytosolic fractions. Protein obtained from different isolation methods (including Percoll alone, Percoll plus MACS, and whole brain tissue lysate) was resolved by 4-12% gradient SDS-PAGE and transferred onto PVDF-FL membranes. The membranes were incubated with primary antibodies (including total OXPHOS Rodent WB antibody Cocktail, anti-α-Tubulin, anti-Synaptophysin, anti-calreticulin, anti-PEX-14, and anti-LAMP1) and then with infrared-labeled secondary antibodies. Percoll-isolated synaptosomal mitochondria retain synaptophysin and calreticulin, while additional purification with the MACS apparatus decreases synaptophysin contamination. The functionality of isolated mitochondria was assessed by measuring respiratory function using a Clark type electrode and OROBOROS Oxygraph-2k. Mitochondrial respiration was recorded in medium containing HEPES and respiratory substrates (malate, glutamate, and succinate). Respiratory control ratios (RCR) were calculated, and oligomycin and FCCP were used to inhibit ATP-synthase and measure maximum respiratory capacity, respectively. The isolated mitochondria responded normally to an addition of ADP and FCCP. The analysis techniques used included immunoblotting, SDS-PAGE, and PVDF-FL membranes for visualization and quantitation. Surgically extracted human cortex samples were used for the experiment, and the purity blots were limited to whole brain samples, non-synaptosomal mitochondria, and MACS-purified synaptosomal mitochondria.

Chunk # 4.
Both mitochondrial 
preparations yield functional mitochondria even though there is a slight loss of respiratory 
capacity with the longer isolation time Figure 3,B shows the respiratory control ratios (RCR) of the isolated mitochondria over 15 hours 
time period following the completion of the isolation protocol The mitochondria were kept on 
ice at 4°C overnight before RCR was tested again at 15 hours and showed retention of 
functionality of the isolated mitochondria It is also important to note that higher concentration of 
mitochondria contributes to their viability Mitochondrial import assay
Mitochondrial matrix protein import requires both presence of the mitochondrial membrane 
potential (which indicates the structural integrity of the mitochondria and the activity of the 
mitochondrial respiratory chain) and functional activity of the mitochondrial enzymes involved 
into the protein uptake and processing Thus, it is an ideal assay to measure the integrity and 
functionality of isolated mitochondria  A pre-ornithine transcarbamoylase (pOTC) cDNA in 
pGEM-3Zf(+)-pOTC plasmid  was transcribed and translated in vitro using the TNT® T7 
Coupled Transcription/Translation- system (Promega, cat no L1170) in the presence of L-[35S]-
methionine (Perkin Elmer, cat no NEG709A500UC) Following translation, [35S]-methionine-
labeled pOTC was incubated with isolated mitochondria at 25 °C for different time points as 
shown, and mitochondria containing imported OTC were collected by centrifugation (9000 g for 
10 min at 4 °C) and separated using SDS-PAGE The radioactively labeled protein were 
visualized by exposing dried gel to a phospho screen (Bio-Rad) and scanned on PMITM 
Personal Molecular Imager system (Bio-Rad) Cleaved mature OTC (mOTC), which represents 
the completion of import into the mitochondrial matrix, and uncleaved OTC (pOTC), were 
quantified using the provided software (Quantity One, version 4.6.9, Bio-Rad) Cleaved mOTC 
(35.2 kDa) has a smaller molecular weight than pOTC (39.9 kDa) and therefore migrates faster 
on the gel This allowed the quantification of the efficiency of the mitochondrial import 
machinery based on the ratio of mOTC to pOTC Figure 3 represents the typical activity of 
mitochondrial protein import tested in vitro The lower bands represent cleaved mOTC and show 
conclusively that the isolated mitochondria are able to import proteins into the matrix Non-
synaptosomal mitochondria have been used as a reference for import activity(Yano et al 2014) Page 10 of 17Accepted Manuscript
10Synaptosomal mitochondria isolated using the traditional Percoll method or our new method, 
show decreased import activity compared to non-synaptosomal mitochondria This is attributed 
to the exposure of these mitochondria to 1,100-psi pressure in the cell disruption vessel as well 
as the prolonged isolation time In comparing synaptosomal mitochondria isolated using both 
aforementioned methods, there is a mild non-significant (60 min time point, Student’s t-test, 
p=0.1175) decrease in import activity when the mitochondria are isolated using our method 
Given that the functionality of the mitochondria is intact, any further functional or structural 
studies to examine differences between neuronal and non-neuronal mitochondria can be 
conducted  
Conclusion
With this method, we demonstrate the ability to isolate highly enriched and purified functional 
neuronal mitochondria from human brain This method represents a significant advance over 
existing methods that result in highly enriched and purified mitochondria not specific to neurons, 
or neuronal mitochondrial fractions contaminated with synaptosomal vesicles, endoplasmic 
reticulum, lysosomes, peroxisomes or other cellular debris The use of our method allows 
investigators to probe both structural and functional properties of neuronal mitochondria without 
interactions from other cellular contaminants It is important to note that our method will 
eliminate any mitochondria-endoplasmic reticulum domains and therefore makes it unsuitable 
for any experiments studying those microdomains Acknowledgements

summary:
1. Both mitochondrial preparations yield functional mitochondria despite a slight loss of respiratory capacity with longer isolation time.
2. The respiratory control ratios (RCR) of the isolated mitochondria were tested over a 15-hour period, showing retention of functionality.
3. Higher concentration of mitochondria contributes to their viability.
4. The mitochondrial matrix protein import assay is used to measure the integrity and functionality of isolated mitochondria.
5. The assay involves the presence of mitochondrial membrane potential and functional activity of mitochondrial enzymes.
6. A pre-ornithine transcarbamoylase (pOTC) cDNA is transcribed and translated in vitro for the import assay.
7. Cleaved mature OTC (mOTC) and uncleaved OTC (pOTC) are quantified to determine the efficiency of mitochondrial import.
8. Non-synaptosomal mitochondria serve as a reference for import activity.
9. Synaptosomal mitochondria isolated using the traditional Percoll method or the new method show decreased import activity compared to non-synaptosomal mitochondria.
10. Our method of isolating neuronal mitochondria allows for structural and functional studies without contamination from other cellular debris.
11. The method eliminates mitochondria-endoplasmic reticulum domains and is not suitable for studying those microdomains.
12. The study was supported by US National Institutes of Health grants and other foundations.
13. References are provided for further reading on mitochondrial viability and calcium-induced reactive oxygen species generation in brain mitochondria.

**Article:** Cyclophilin D-sensitive mitochondrial permeability transition in adult human brain and liver mitochondria

**Section:** Materials and Methods

**1. Extract Steps:**
1. Brain tissue samples were collected from adult human brains.
2. Mitochondria were isolated from brain tissue using a Percoll density gradient centrifugation method.
3. Mitochondrial protein concentration was determined using the Bradford assay.
4. Mitochondrial purity was assessed using immunoblotting.
5. Respiration of isolated non-synaptosomal (NS) and synaptosomal (Syn) mitochondria was measured.
6. Mitochondrial functional stability was tested by keeping isolated mitochondria on ice and measuring respiration after different time periods.
7. Mitochondria isolated using the traditional Percoll method and the new method (Syn MACS) were compared for functional assays.
8. Mitochondria isolated from extracted human cortex were used for pOTC import assays.

**2. Note Incompleteness:**

summary:
Summary 7:
This study investigates the role of cyclophilin D-sensitive mitochondrial permeability transition in adult human brain and liver mitochondria. It explores the redox regulation of autophagy in healthy brain and neurodegeneration and highlights the potential of mitochondrial permeability transition as a target for neuroprotection. The study also focuses on the inhibition of mitochondrial cytochrome c release and functional deficits after spinal cord injury. Additionally, it examines the therapeutic potential of inhibitors of cytochrome c release for Huntington's disease. The researchers discuss the inhibition of mitochondrial protein import by mutant huntingtin and the protective effects of nortriptyline in cerebral ischemia/hypoxia injury. They also investigate the inhibitory effects of melatonin on the caspase-1/cytochrome c/caspase-3 cell death pathway and its role in delaying disease progression in a mouse model of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Furthermore, the study explores the neuroprotective effects of N-acetyl-serotonin through the inhibition of mitochondrial death pathways and autophagic activation in experimental models of ischemic injury. It emphasizes the potential of minocycline as an inhibitor of cytochrome c release and a delay in the progression of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. The researchers present figures illustrating the mitochondrial isolation technique, the assessment of purity using their method, respiration of isolated mitochondria, and import assays of mitochondria from the human cortex. The study highlights the importance of cyclophilin D-sensitive mitochondrial permeability transition in neurodegeneration and spinal cord injury. It also emphasizes the role of redox regulation of autophagy in healthy brain and neurodegeneration. The therapeutic potential of inhibiting mitochondrial cytochrome c release is discussed for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. Melatonin is shown to delay disease progression in ALS through the inhibition of the caspase-1/cytochrome c/caspase-3 cell death pathway. N-acetyl-serotonin offers neuroprotection by inhibiting mitochondrial death pathways and autophagic activation in ischemic injury models. Minocycline is found to delay the progression of ALS by inhibiting cytochrome c release.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Centrifugation at 16,700 g for 10 min to remove brain tissue and blood.
2. Homogenization with a glass Teflon potter at 400 rpm for 20 strokes.
3. Centrifugation at 1,300 g for 3 min, repeated twice, to obtain the supernatant.
4. Centrifugation at 21,000 g for 10 min to obtain the pellet of crude brain mitochondria.
5. Percoll step-gradient centrifugation at 30,700 g for 8 min to separate nonsynaptosomal mitochondria (band between 24 and 40% Percoll) and synaptosomes (band in the 24% Percoll).
6. Cell disruption using a vessel at 1100 psi for 15 min to obtain crude synaptosomal mitochondria.
7. Immuno-purification using anti-TOM22 antibody conjugated to super-magnetic microbeads.
8. Centrifugation at 8000 g for 10 min to obtain the pellet of nonsynaptosomal or synaptosomal mitochondria.
9. Re-suspend and incubate the pellet with anti-TOM22 microbeads for 60 min at a mitochondria concentration of 0.3 mg/mL.
10. Load the suspension with immune-labeled mitochondria onto a pre-equilibrated MACS Column mounted on the MACS Separator.
11. Wash the MACS Column mounted on the MACS Separator three times.
12. Elute the mitochondria from the MACS column to obtain a suspension of pure immuno-labeled mitochondria.
13. Centrifugation at 13,000 g for 4 min to obtain the pellet of purified synaptosomal mitochondria.
14. Resuspend the pellet in Isolation Buffer II.
15. Percoll step-gradient centrifugation at 30,700 g for 10 min to obtain the pellet.
16. Centrifugation at 6900 g for 10 min to obtain the final suspension of purified synaptosomal mitochondria.

**Incompleteness:** The article does not provide details on the isolation buffer composition or the specific volumes used in each step. The time and temperature for each centrifugation step are also not mentioned.

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed protocol for isolating synaptosomal mitochondria using a combination of centrifugation, Percoll step-gradient centrifugation, cell disruption, and immuno-purification. However, there are gaps in the protocol, such as missing buffer composition and volumes, as well as time and temperature details for centrifugation steps.
